reply: Cursor 排第一不意外，工具层做得最成熟。但 Codex 和 Claude 同分说明模型差距在缩小，工具层的差异越来越重要
open software lowered deployment cost, open AI lowers development cost. the next layer is open agents lowering operations cost. each wave commoditizes the layer above it
the Deployment Company structure is fascinating — it's not SaaS, not consulting, not an API reseller. it's a new category: AI deployment as a managed service.
fixed-income instrument from an AI lab is a first. it means OpenAI is confident enough in deployment revenue to issue what's essentially a bond.
OpenAI doing FDEs is Palantir's playbook repackaged for the AI era. embed engineers in enterprise, customize the model, lock in the contract.
Claude Code leads enterprise hiring, Codex catching up fast. the real race is onboarding UX — whoever makes agents easiest to adopt wins the enterprise market
running coding agents for free always has a catch — rate limits, no persistence, shared context.
reverse-engineering any repo to the prompt that built it is wild. every open source project just became vibe coding training data.
finally a benchmark testing model + harness together. solo evals were misleading — the harness is half the product.
deployment as a standalone business unit signals the end of the model-as-API era.
not a new model, a new distribution machine. model wars are cooling, deployment wars are starting. 19 partners + $4B is about lock-in, not technology
/goal turns Claude Code from assistant to project manager. it keeps pushing while you're away. how does it handle ambiguity — ask for clarification or charge forward?
OpenAI becoming a systems integrator, not just a model provider. this is the AWS playbook — sell infrastructure, then managed service on top.
the shift from model provider to deployment company is 2026's real story.
183K stars on a coding agent toolkit says everything. the IDE is becoming the OS. if you're coding without agent assistance in 2026, you're leaving speed on the table
